name: Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot — The Lavish Outlier (c. 1442)
description:
# Cary-Yale Tarot (Visconti di Modrone)

## At a glance

An even earlier, extravagantly expanded Milanese game deck — possibly the oldest tarot
fragment of all — notable for **six** ranks of court cards and for trumps depicting the
three **theological virtues** (Faith, Hope, Charity) found almost nowhere else. A
court **game** object, again from the Visconti world, again with no divinatory use.

## Origin & dating

Held at Yale's Beinecke Library (the "Cary" of Cary-Yale; also called *Visconti di
Modrone*). Giordano Berti dates it **c.1442–1447** on numismatic grounds: the coin
(*denari*) cards reproduce the gold florin struck by Filippo Maria Visconti in 1442 and
withdrawn at his death in 1447. If correct, this is among the very earliest surviving
tarot material.

## Provenance & evidence

A fragmentary set (surviving cards number in the dozens). Its structural oddities are the
key evidence: **six courts** (King, Queen, Knight, Jack plus a "Damsel" and a mounted
"Lady"), and **theological-virtue trumps** that otherwise appear only in the much later
Minchiate. This suggests the early trump set was not yet standardised — different courts
experimented with how many triumphs to include.

## Game or divination?

**Game.** The expansions are about enriching play and flattering a dynasty, not encoding
secret doctrine. The virtue cards are conventional Christian moral allegory, the common
visual currency of Renaissance courts.

## The fear question

**No fear** — same logic as the Visconti-Sforza. Elite, heraldic, openly commissioned.
If anything the Cary-Yale is *more* ostentatious (more gold, more courts), the opposite
of something hidden.

## Counter-voices

Because the Cary-Yale shares the theological virtues with Minchiate, some esoteric
readers treat it as evidence of a lost "complete" sacred system later censored down to
22 trumps. The simpler, better-supported reading is the reverse: the early trump set was
**unfixed**, and standardisation (not censorship) trimmed it over time.

## Open questions / corrections owed

- Whether the virtues *replaced* standard trumps or were *additional* is unsettled.
- The c.1442 date leans on the florin argument; not all scholars accept it as decisive.

## Sources & further reading

- "Cary-Yale Visconti Tarocchi Deck," learntarot.com; Beinecke Library highlights.
- "Tarocchi Visconti di Modrone (Cary-Yale)," Tarot Heritage (Berti dating).
- Dummett, *The Game of Tarot* (1980).

**RESEARCH NOTE (2026-06-13).** **67 cards survive of ~86 original.** The six-rank courts (adding a Damsel and a Mounted Lady to King/Queen/Knight/Page) plus theological-virtue trumps make this the great outlier. NB a modelling issue: the extra court ranks currently collide on a four-archetype scheme (Damsel & Knave → page; Mounted Lady & Knight → knight) — needs distinct archetype ids. Bembo vs Zavattari attribution remains debated. See research/decks/cary-yale-visconti-tarot.md.

**EDITORIAL CALL (2026-06-13, maintainer-delegated):** resolved the six-rank-court archetype collision — the **Damsel** now keys to `card:damsel-of-<suit>` and the **Mounted Lady/Horsewoman** to `card:horsewoman-of-<suit>` (was colliding with Page/Knight). 7 court cards re-keyed.

sections.Iconography: An enthroned, bearded emperor in gold brocade holding an orb, the imperial eagle on display, attendants at his feet.
sections.History: One of the few trumps to survive in this deck. The Cary-Yale was likely made for the 1441 marriage of Bianca Maria Visconti and Francesco Sforza; its emperor is dynastic propaganda in gold.
sections.Reading: Temporal authority and the architecture of rule — the masculine throne the whole procession serves.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Scene: The Emperor of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot is a crowned male sovereign enthroned or standing against a gold-leaf background, painted by Bonifacio Bembo in the mid-15th century for the Visconti court of Milan. He wears the elaborate robes and armor of an Italian Renaissance ruler — likely reflecting the imagery of Filippo Maria Visconti or the early Sforza dynasty. The figure holds a scepter or orb, the attributes of temporal dominion, and is surrounded by the heraldic splendor of the ducal court. The gold ground and delicate tempera painting reflect the luxury production values of early tarot as an aristocratic gift object.
sections.Symbol: The Visconti Emperor — crowned temporal authority against gold, painted for the ducal court of Milan as the embodiment of imperial sovereignty.

sections.History: A surviving trump. The Cary-Yale's courtly women are rendered with unusual individuality — part of the deck's preoccupation with the feminine that also produced its female knights and pages.
sections.Reading: Sovereign abundance, the generative authority that rules from the throne.

sections.Scene: The Empress of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot is a crowned imperial woman enthroned or standing against a gold-leaf background. She wears the elaborate courtly dress of a 15th-century Italian noblewoman, her regalia reflecting the prestige of the Visconti-Sforza family for whom this deck was painted. She holds the scepter or orb of female sovereignty, her presence the counterpart of the Emperor — temporal authority in its feminine expression. The gold ground and refined tempera technique by Bonifacio Bembo make this one of the most opulent representations of the Empress in tarot history.
sections.Symbol: The Visconti Empress — female imperial authority in courtly splendor, the feminine sovereign of the earliest surviving luxury tarot deck.

sections.History: Among the most celebrated surviving Cary-Yale cards — very probably a portrait of the Visconti-Sforza wedding the deck was made to honour.
sections.Reading: Union as alliance, love bound to lineage — desire formalised into a marriage of houses.

sections.Scene: The Lovers of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot shows a courtly scene of two figures — a man and a woman — in the act of a formal betrothal or amorous union, often with a Cupid or binding figure above. The image reflects the aristocratic culture of courtly love and dynastic marriage that characterized the Visconti-Sforza court. The gold-leaf background and the exquisite detail of the painted costumes make this card a document of the fashion and ceremony of 15th-century Milanese court life.
sections.Symbol: The Courtly Betrothal — two figures in formal union under Cupid's blessing, dynastic love as a social and ceremonial act in the Visconti court's world.

sections.Iconography: A crowned noblewoman rides a canopied triumphal car drawn by two white horses, attendants walking alongside.
sections.History: The card that names the game (trionfo / triumph). The Cary-Yale chariot is driven by a woman, in keeping with the deck's foregrounding of female figures.
sections.Reading: Triumph and momentum — the self borne forward in a victory parade over what it has mastered.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Scene: The Chariot of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot shows a victorious figure in a triumphal car — a chariot drawn by horses or other animals, the sovereign or allegorical figure riding above the world in a posture of victory. The image reflects the tradition of the Roman triumph (trionfi) which gave the tarot trumps their name, the allegorical pageant of victory that was a central feature of Italian Renaissance court celebration.
sections.Symbol: The Triumphal Car — victory in motion, the Roman trionfo tradition giving tarot its name embodied in the earliest surviving luxury deck.

sections.Iconography: A figure in courtly dress overpowers a lion, wrenching open or subduing its jaws — the Herculean image of fortitude.
sections.History: A surviving cardinal virtue. In the Cary-Yale the cardinal virtues sit alongside the rare theological virtues (Faith, Hope, Charity) — an unusually complete moral programme.
sections.Reading: Fortitude — strength of soul that masters the beast through steady will rather than rage.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Scene: The Strength (Fortezza) of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot shows the allegorical virtue of Fortitude — possibly as a woman breaking a column or restraining a lion, the conventional iconography of this virtue in Italian medieval and Renaissance art. The Cary-Yale's Strength is one of three cardinal virtues surviving in this deck (alongside Faith, Hope, and Charity as theological virtues), making this an unusually virtue-rich early tarot.
sections.Symbol: Fortitude subduing the column or lion — one of the surviving virtues of the Cary-Yale, the virtue of moral strength in its classical allegorical form.

sections.Reading: The great leveller — the end that rides through every estate without exception.

sections.Scene: The Death card of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot shows the skeletal figure of Death as the universal harvester — though in the Cary-Yale's distinctive Bembo style, the figure is rendered with the technical refinement and dignity of aristocratic painting rather than the macabre grotesquerie of the danse macabre tradition. Against the gold ground, Death moves through the world as an allegorical presence from the same tradition as Petrarch's Triumph of Death.
sections.Symbol: The Aristocratic Death — mortality rendered in the refined Bembo style against gold leaf, death as an allegorical triumph in the Petrarchan tradition.

sections.Reading: The call and the reckoning — awakening, summons, the dead rising to answer.

sections.Scene: The Judgement of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot shows the angel of the Last Judgment sounding the trumpet above the risen dead — the conventional Christian eschatological scene rendered in the aristocratic style of 15th-century Milanese painting. The gold ground and fine tempera make this card a devotional object as much as a gaming piece, the divine judgment depicted with the reverence appropriate to a luxury commission for the Visconti court.
sections.Symbol: The Divine Summons — the trumpet angel above the risen dead, eschatological judgment painted in the aristocratic devotional style of 15th-century Milan.

sections.History: The highest triumph. The Cary-Yale's World is more a panorama of earthly glory than the later cosmic wreath — fitting for a dynastic deck.
sections.Reading: Completion and dominion — the whole world held up to be seen and possessed.

sections.Scene: The World of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot shows the cosmic completion of the triumph sequence — likely a figure or allegorical form representing the world as a whole, possibly with the mandorla (almond-shaped halo) that surrounds the feminine personification of the world in later Marseille tradition. The World is the apex of the surviving Cary-Yale trumps, the final triumph that crowns the allegorical sequence of the earliest surviving tarot deck.
sections.Symbol: The World as Cosmic Triumph — the apex of the Cary-Yale's allegorical sequence, completion rendered in the Bembo luxury style against gold.

sections.Iconography: A woman holds a chalice and cross, sometimes with a kneeling figure — the personification of Faith (Fides).
sections.History: One of the three THEOLOGICAL virtues found in no other tarot. Their presence suggests the Cary-Yale was conceived as a complete moral cosmos — the four cardinal plus the three theological virtues.
sections.Reading: Faith — trust that orients the soul beyond what it can prove, the first of the theological virtues.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Scene: Faith (Fede) is a theological virtue card unique to the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot — she does not appear as a separate trump in any standard later tarot deck. The figure of Faith is depicted as a woman with the attributes of Christian faith: possibly a chalice, a cross, or the Apostles' Creed scroll, or a figure holding a candle in darkness. Against the gold-leaf background of Bonifacio Bembo's luxury tempera painting, Faith stands as a theological complement to the three Cardinal Virtues (Fortitude, Justice, Temperance) that appear in most later tarots.
sections.Symbol: Faith as Trump — the theological virtue of believing what cannot be seen, unique to the Cary-Yale and absent from all later standard tarot decks.

sections.Iconography: A woman gazes upward with hands clasped in prayer, often with an anchor — the personification of Hope (Spes).
sections.History: A theological virtue unique to this deck. The anchor is Hope's ancient attribute; here it joins the tarot for the only time in its history.
sections.Reading: Hope — the upward reach that endures through the dark, the anchor of the soul.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Scene: Hope (Speranza) is a theological virtue card unique to the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot. The figure of Hope is typically depicted as a woman with her hands raised toward heaven or reaching upward toward a divine anchor — the classical attributes of the virtue that sustains the soul in the absence of present certainty. Against the gold ground of the Bembo tempera, Hope stands as the second of the three theological virtues that distinguish this deck from all subsequent tarot traditions.
sections.Symbol: Hope as Trump — the theological virtue of reaching toward what is not yet present, the second of three virtues unique to the Cary-Yale.

sections.Iconography: A woman nurses or tends children, sometimes with a flame — the personification of Charity (Caritas), greatest of the three.
sections.History: The third and greatest theological virtue, unique to the Cary-Yale. Charity nursing children is the classic Caritas image carried over from devotional painting.
sections.Reading: Charity — love that gives itself away, named by tradition the greatest of the virtues.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Scene: Charity (Carità) is the third theological virtue unique to the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot, completing the trio of faith, hope, and charity from the First Letter to the Corinthians. The figure of Charity is depicted as a woman in the act of giving — distributing gifts, nurturing children at her breast, or holding her heart toward others. Against the gold-leaf background of the Cary-Yale's luxury production, Charity is the virtue that grounds the theological trio in concrete action toward the other.
sections.Symbol: Charity as Trump — the greatest of the theological virtues according to Paul, giving and nurturing as the active expression of divine love, unique to the Cary-Yale.

sections.Scene: The Ace of Coins of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot shows a single large coin or disc against the card's field — the concentrated emblem of the suit in its undivided potential. The Cary-Yale's pip cards follow the Italian playing card tradition of showing suit symbols in simple geometric or heraldic arrangements, against the gold-touched or patterned backgrounds that reflect the deck's luxury production values.
sections.Symbol: A single coin — the undivided material potential of the suit, the seed of earthly prosperity in the earliest surviving luxury tarot.

sections.Scene: The Queen of Batons or Staves is an enthroned female figure bearing the baton suit's emblem in the elaborate dress of a 15th-century Italian noblewoman. Against the gold-leaf background of the Cary-Yale's luxury tempera, the Queen of Batons represents the high feminine authority of the creative suit — warmth, generosity, and creative power expressed through the aristocratic feminine ideal of the Visconti-Sforza court.
sections.Symbol: The Queen of Batons enthroned — the high feminine authority of the creative suit, a Milanese duchess bearing the staff against gold.

sections.About these Cards: Only a handful of the Cary-Yale's triumphs survive: the Emperor, Empress, Lovers, Chariot, Strength, Death, Judgement, and the World. The rest are lost. What remains is enough to show a deck of extraordinary richness — full gold grounds, individualised faces, dynastic pride.
sections.How to Use: Read these as the fragments of a once-complete procession of estates, made to celebrate a ducal marriage.
sections.Scene: The surviving trumps of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot form an incomplete but historically precious group: Emperor, Empress, Lovers, Chariot, Strength, Death, Judgement, World — eight cards of what may have been eleven or more trumps in the original deck. These eight cards represent the earliest surviving evidence of the tarot allegorical sequence as a painted luxury object, commissioned for the Visconti-Sforza court of Milan in the mid-15th century.
sections.Symbol: The eight surviving Cary-Yale trumps — an incomplete but irreplaceable window into the earliest luxury tarot, the allegorical sequence at the beginning of the tradition.

items.name: The Theological Virtues — Faith, Hope, Charity

sections.About these Cards: The Cary-Yale alone among tarots adds the three THEOLOGICAL virtues — Faith, Hope, and Charity — to the usual cardinal virtues. No other tarot deck contains them. They turn the deck into a complete medieval moral cosmos.
sections.Why this Matters: These three cards are the single strongest sign that the early tarot trumps were conceived as a moral and cosmological ladder, not a random gallery — and a reminder of how much the 'standard' 22-trump sequence is a later simplification.
sections.Scene: The three Theological Virtue cards (Faith, Hope, Charity) form a group unique to the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot among all surviving tarot decks. Placed alongside the Cardinal Virtues (Fortitude survives, while Justice and Temperance are among the lost cards), these three cards reflect the explicitly Christian devotional context of the earliest Milanese tarot. No later standard tarot deck includes these virtues as separate trump cards.
sections.Symbol: Faith, Hope, and Charity as separate trumps — the three theological virtues of Corinthians 13 as unique trump cards, the explicitly Christian devotional element of the earliest luxury tarot.

items.name: Suit of Coins — with the Six-Rank Court

sections.About this Suit: The surviving Coins cards of the Cary-Yale. Like all four suits here, its court runs to six ranks — King, Queen, Knight, Horsewoman, Knave, Damsel — doubling the usual nobility with female riders and pages.
sections.How to Use: Browse the suit to see the Cary-Yale's defining peculiarity: a court half made of women, unmatched in any other historical tarot.
sections.Scene: The coins suit of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot comprises the surviving pip cards (ace, 2, 4-10; with the three apparently lost) and five court ranks: Damsel, Knight, Horsewoman, Queen, King. sections.Symbol: The Cary-Yale coins suit — pip arrangements and five court ranks, the material suit rendered in 15th-century luxury tempera.

sections.Scene: A keyword grouping for the three Theological Virtue trump cards (Faith, Hope, Charity) that are unique to the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot. The theological virtues of the Pauline epistles (1 Corinthians 13) are represented as separate allegorical trump figures alongside the Cardinal Virtues — a feature that distinguishes the Cary-Yale from every other surviving tarot deck and reflects the explicitly Christian devotional context of the earliest Milanese tarot.
sections.Symbol: The Theological Virtues — Faith, Hope, and Charity as separate trump cards, the Christian devotional dimension unique to the Cary-Yale's expanded trump sequence.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “three” (3).
sections.Scene: A keyword grouping for all three-pip cards across the suits of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot. The threes show each suit symbol three times in the triangular or vertical arrangement of the Italian playing card tradition, representing the first triangular expansion of each suit's potential. Note that the three of coins does not appear to survive in the Cary-Yale.
sections.Symbol: Threes — all surviving three-pip cards, the triangular first expansion of each suit's potential.

sections.Description: Cross-cutting facets derived from shared keywords — the same card appears under every keyword it carries.
sections.Scene: The keyword axis of the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot organizes the deck's 105 items by thematic grouping — suits, elements, pip numbers, court ranks, and special categories (trumps, theological virtues, the deck's unique features). This axis allows navigation across the deck through the conceptual patterns that cut across the suit and trump organization, revealing the structural principles of the earliest surviving luxury tarot.
sections.Symbol: The keyword axis — a thematic cross-index of the Cary-Yale's items, the conceptual map of the earliest luxury tarot's structural organization.
